title: Wonton-Style Dumpling Soup

ingredients.name: all-purpose flour (maida), for wrappers
visual_checks: makes about 24 thin wrappers; ready-made wonton sheets from Chinese groceries save time
ingredients.notes: makes about 24 thin wrappers; ready-made wonton sheets from Chinese groceries save time

subs.name: ready-made wonton sheets or spring roll wrappers cut into smaller squares
subs.notes: frozen dumpling wrappers from Chinese grocery shops are a great time-saver

subs.name: minced mushroom (200 g) + minced cabbage (100 g) for a vegetarian version
subs.notes: squeeze out all moisture from mushroom and cabbage before mixing

ingredients.name: chicken or vegetable stock
visual_checks: a good stock is the heart of this soup
ingredients.notes: a good stock is the heart of this soup

subs.name: water with 2 stock cubes + 1 tsp soy sauce
subs.notes: a rich broth makes a significant difference here

steps.text: Make the wrapper dough: combine the flour with a generous pinch of salt. Add about 90 ml of just-boiled water gradually, mixing with a spoon, then knead for 5 minutes until you have a smooth, firm dough. It should not stick to your hands. Cover with a damp cloth and rest for 20 minutes.

Meanwhile, prepare the filling: combine minced chicken with 1 tablespoon soy sauce, the sesame oil, ginger, garlic, spring onions and pepper. Mix thoroughly in one direction until the mixture becomes slightly sticky. Taste by cooking a tiny amount in a dry pan and adjust seasoning.

Divide the dough into 4 portions. Roll each portion on a lightly floured surface as thin as you can — aim for 1–2 mm thick. Cut into 8 cm squares. Dust the squares lightly with cornflour to prevent sticking.

To fold each wonton: place 1 heaped teaspoon of filling in the centre of a wrapper. Wet the edges with a fingertip dipped in water. Fold over into a triangle, pressing out any air and sealing firmly. Bring the two bottom corners together and pinch to seal. Dust the finished wontons with cornflour.
Make the broth: heat the stock in a large pot with 3 slices of ginger and the remaining soy sauce. Bring to a gentle simmer and season with salt. Keep warm on low heat.

Cook the wontons in a separate large pot of boiling water. Drop them in batches of 8. They are done when they float and the skin looks slightly translucent, about 4–5 minutes. Remove with a slotted spoon.
Divide the cooked wontons among bowls (about 6 per person). Ladle the hot broth generously over them. Drizzle with a little extra sesame oil, top with sliced spring onions and serve immediately.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: keema → minced mushroom (200 g) + minced cabbage (100 g) for a vegetarian version (squeeze out all moisture from mushroom and cabbage before mixing); maida → ready-made wonton sheets or spring roll wrappers cut into smaller squares (frozen dumpling wrappers from Chinese grocery shops are a great time-saver); stock → water with 2 stock cubes + 1 tsp soy sauce (a rich broth makes a significant difference here); chicken → prawn (jhinga) for a more traditional Cantonese filling (chop prawns roughly; do not mince them completely for a better texture)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Maida (all-purpose flour) is your wrapper dough — the same flour used for making poori and paratha. Minced chicken (keema) is available from most butchers. Wonton skins can also be bought frozen from Chinese grocery stores in metro cities. For the broth, use a good store-bought stock cube dissolved in hot water with fresh ginger. Gingelly oil (nallennai) is your sesame oil. The folding requires only practice — the seal is more important than the shape.
